issue: Natchitoches Parish, LA Consolidated SD No. 11, GO School Bonds, Series 2026

source_excerpt:
[PAGE 1]
Research Update: 
Natchitoches Parish School District Nos. 7, 8, And 9, 
LA GO Debt Rating Outlook Revised To Negative On 
Deficits 
March 26, 2026
Overview
•S&P Global Ratings revised the outlook to negative from stable on Natchitoches Parish School 
Districts No. 7, No. 8, and No. 9, La.’s general obligation (GO) debt, and affirmed its ‘A’ rating on 
the debt. 
•S&P Global Ratings also assigned its ‘A’ rating, with a negative outlook, to Natchitoches Parish 
School District No. 11, La.’s $12.5 million series 2026 GO school bonds. 
•The outlook revision to negative reflects a deterioration of the districts’ reserves resulting 
from deficits in the past two fiscal years. 
Rationale
Security
We base our rating on the application of our "Issue Credit Ratings Linked To U.S. Public Finance 
Obligors' Creditworthiness" criteria (Nov. 20, 2019). Natchitoches Parish School Board is the 
governing and operating authority for all four districts, which represent a portion of the school 
board's boundaries and exist solely as taxing entities, including for the issuance and repayment 
of locally voted GO bonds, secured by unlimited ad valorem taxes levied on all taxable property 
within the boundaries of the districts. We view each school district as having very similar credit 
quality, differing slightly only in certain economic and debt ratios. In addition, the management 
and associated operations for the districts are identical. Although the ad valorem taxes from 
each district are derived from a subset of the school board's tax base, we do not view the school 
districts’ taxing bases as being exposed to disproportionate risks relative to the school board. 
Therefore, we rate the bonds as on par with our view of the school board's general 
creditworthiness. The school board plans on using bond proceeds for the 2026 issuance for 
capital projects in District 11.
Credit highlights

[PAGE 2]
Our rating reflects the school board’s recent financial performance, uncertainty related to the 
potential loss of assessed value due to the departure of a major employer in the parish, a 
concentrated local economy, and manageable long-term liabilities. In the past two years, school 
board officials stated that reserves have been spent down due to one-time related expenditures 
related to construction projects; as well, some budgeted revenues did not materialize. In 
response, school board officials cut 78 positions over the past two fiscal years, with an estimated 
$5 million savings a year, and have closed two schools, with preliminary discussions to close 
another two or three. The school board is expecting positive results for fiscal 2026 due to the 
closure of the schools and staff reductions.
In addition to the changes the school district faces with staffing, management has to deal with 
potential challenges around the closure of an International Paper facility in the district. At 
present, officials are unsure how the closure will affect property tax revenue. Reassessment of 
value in the parish is slated for 2028, and although officials believe the property value will not 
decline immediately given the building facility, they are uncertain by how much, if any, the value 
will drop, and have indicated the facility might be repurposed.
The parish reported multiple economic developments in various manufacturing and commercial 
facilities. In addition, Haynesville shale has experienced a recent uptick in activity with new leases 
signed. A new solar project is also coming to the parish. Northwestern State University, which is a 
significant presence in the parish, also reported increased enrollment, the opening of a new 
building, and the development of a sport nutrition performance center.

pages.text:
Research Update: 
Natchitoches Parish School District Nos. 7, 8, And 9, 
LA GO Debt Rating Outlook Revised To Negative On 
Deficits 
March 26, 2026
Overview
•S&P Global Ratings revised the outlook to negative from stable on Natchitoches Parish School 
Districts No. 7, No. 8, and No. 9, La.’s general obligation (GO) debt, and affirmed its ‘A’ rating on 
the debt. 
•S&P Global Ratings also assigned its ‘A’ rating, with a negative outlook, to Natchitoches Parish 
School District No. 11, La.’s $12.5 million series 2026 GO school bonds. 
•The outlook revision to negative reflects a deterioration of the districts’ reserves resulting 
from deficits in the past two fiscal years. 
Rationale
Security
We base our rating on the application of our "Issue Credit Ratings Linked To U.S. Public Finance 
Obligors' Creditworthiness" criteria (Nov. 20, 2019). Natchitoches Parish School Board is the 
governing and operating authority for all four districts, which represent a portion of the school 
board's boundaries and exist solely as taxing entities, including for the issuance and repayment 
of locally voted GO bonds, secured by unlimited ad valorem taxes levied on all taxable property 
within the boundaries of the districts. We view each school district as having very similar credit 
quality, differing slightly only in certain economic and debt ratios. In addition, the management 
and associated operations for the districts are identical. Although the ad valorem taxes from 
each district are derived from a subset of the school board's tax base, we do not view the school 
districts’ taxing bases as being exposed to disproportionate risks relative to the school board. 
Therefore, we rate the bonds as on par with our view of the school board's general 
creditworthiness. The school board plans on using bond proceeds for the 2026 issuance for 
capital projects in District 11.
Credit highlights
Primary Contact
Alex Louie
Englewood 
1-303-721-4559
alex.louie
@spglobal.com
Secondary Contact
Lauren Levy
Englewood 
+  1303 721 4956
lauren.levy
@spglobal.com
www.spglobal.com/ratingsdirect March 26, 2026       1

Our rating reflects the school board’s recent financial performance, uncertainty related to the 
potential loss of assessed value due to the departure of a major employer in the parish, a 
concentrated local economy, and manageable long-term liabilities. In the past two years, school 
board officials stated that reserves have been spent down due to one-time related expenditures 
related to construction projects; as well, some budgeted revenues did not materialize. In 
response, school board officials cut 78 positions over the past two fiscal years, with an estimated 
$5 million savings a year, and have closed two schools, with preliminary discussions to close 
another two or three. The school board is expecting positive results for fiscal 2026 due to the 
closure of the schools and staff reductions.
In addition to the changes the school district faces with staffing, management has to deal with 
potential challenges around the closure of an International Paper facility in the district. At 
present, officials are unsure how the closure will affect property tax revenue. Reassessment of 
value in the parish is slated for 2028, and although officials believe the property value will not 
decline immediately given the building facility, they are uncertain by how much, if any, the value 
will drop, and have indicated the facility might be repurposed.
The parish reported multiple economic developments in various manufacturing and commercial 
facilities. In addition, Haynesville shale has experienced a recent uptick in activity with new leases 
signed. A new solar project is also coming to the parish. Northwestern State University, which is a 
significant presence in the parish, also reported increased enrollment, the opening of a new 
building, and the development of a sport nutrition performance center.
The rating also reflects our view of the following for the school board:
•Per capita gross county product for Natchitoches Parish is 61.0% of the U.S. level, and per 
capital personal income is 74.7%, which are at ‘A’ rating level medians. The parish has some 
economic uncertainty due to the recent closure of the International Paper facility. The local 
economy also has multiple industrial developments underway, including from RoyOMartin (a 
wood products company), Weyerhaeuser, and other projects at the Natchitoches Parish Port. 
Northwest State University is also seeing enrollment growth.
•Two years of deficits due to unrealized revenues and one-time expenditures related to 
Elementary and Secondary School Emergency Relief Fund grants from the pandemic. Officials 
have taken actions to reduce staff and close schools and expect a surplus in 2026. District 
enrollment has declined in recent years, and although officials expect continued modest 
declines in the next couple of years, a demographic study indicates enrollment could stabilize 
soon after.
•Reserves are supported by a formal reserve policy of 10% of expenditures. The school board is 
in compliance with this policy at present, but we believe reserves are low given uncertainty 
about property taxes and declining enrollment. In 2026, officials expect to report a surplus and 
add to reserves.
•Conservative management practices, with recent changes at the management level that 
include conservative budget assumptions, monthly budget-to-actual reports that are reviewed 
by the board, an informal investment policy mirroring state guidelines that governs 
investments, and a formal minimum reserve policy (10% of expenditures) that was adopted in 
August 2023. Management is conducting a facilities assessment, and is developing a 
maintenance backlog plan, due in September 2026.
•For more information on our institutional framework assessment for Louisiana municipalities, 
see  “Institutional Framework Assessment: Louisiana Local Governments,” Sept. 9, 2024.
www.spglobal.com/ratingsdirect March 26, 2026       2
Natchitoches Parish School District Nos. 7, 8, And 9, LA GO Debt Rating Outlook Revised To Negative On Deficits

Environmental, social, and governance 
Given the parish's proximity to the Gulf Coast, we consider the district to have a higher exposure 
to acute physical risks within our credit analysis, including severe weather events and 
intermittent flooding, as well as exposure to chronic physical risks, such as sea-level rise, 
extreme heat, and drought. Although typically extreme weather events are temporary, 
recurrence and prolonged exposure to these events can weaken property tax bases, tourism 
activity, and the pace of economic development for affected local governments.
Outlook
The negative outlook reflects at least a one-in-three chance we could lower the ratings on the 
districts if fiscal 2026 results do not align with management expectations despite actions to 
reduce staff and close schools, and if property tax uncertainty from the International Paper 
closure causes continued deficits.
Downside scenario
If fiscal 2026 or fiscal 2027 results show a spenddown of reserves, we could take another 
negative rating action. 
Upside scenario
If the school district can stabilize operations and maintain reserves at a level above the fund 
balance target for multiple years, we could take a positive rating action.

opinion about the creditworthiness of the U.S. Public Finance obligor's legal pledge for payment of the financial obligation. Nevertheless, these maturities may have
